FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data; N=9475 from baseline cohort with annual follow-up assessments beginning 2016

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures smartphone ownership age as specific variable; parents asked each year at what age child received first smartphone

FT_IC3.evidence: Cross-sectional multivariable logistic regression model; reports associations between sociodemographic predictors and smartphone ownership age as binary outcome

FT_IC4.evidence: Table 2 reports ORs with 95% CIs for all predictors (e.g., Female OR=1.38 95% CI 1.19–1.60; Black OR=2.78 95% CI 2.07–3.64)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; cross-sectional logistic regression on ABCD data

FT_EC7_reason: Smartphone ownership age is the DEPENDENT variable in all reported analyses. All predictors are demographic/sociodemographic only (sex, race/ethnicity, household income, parental marital status, parental education). Analysis is cross-sectional (not longitudinal prospective). Per E7/E14 rule: cross-sectional sociodemographic predictors of phone/SM outcome = FT-EC7 applies. EXCLUDE.
notes: Paper focuses on sociodemographic disparities in smartphone ownership timing; no analysis of smartphone/SM as predictor of other outcomes; no individual psychological/clinical/behavioral predictors included.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data; described as 'Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development SM Study'; n=5,639 ages 10–13 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures multiple qualifying SM variables: social media addiction scale (SMA), hours spent per day on SM, most-used platform (TikTok, Instagram, Snapchat), public/secret accounts, follower/following counts

FT_IC3.evidence: Regression models report associations between SMA and SM usage patterns; multiple inferential tests with reported coefficients

FT_IC4.evidence: Abstract reports regression coefficients (b values) with 95% CIs: TikTok b=1.52 95% CI 1.12–1.93; Instagram b=0.92 95% CI 0.51–1.34; Snapchat b=0.60 95% CI 0.16–1.03; public account b=0.85 95% CI 0.56–1.13; secret account b=2.27 95% CI 1.65–2.89; SM hours b=0.66 95% CI 0.61–0.71

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; cross-sectional regression study examining SM usage associations with SMA

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article; published in JAACAP Open 2026

FT_EC7_reason: Paper reports SM variables (hours, platform, accounts) as INDEPENDENT variables predicting SMA (outcome), not the reverse. Even though SMA could be viewed as SM-related, the analysis structure has SM usage patterns predicting addiction scores, with extractable coefficients. This is phone/SM→outcome analysis (analogous to SM use→mental health), so FT-EC7 does NOT apply. INCLUDE.
notes: No PDF available; screening based on abstract only. Abstract provides sufficient detail on methods and results including specific coefficients.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data; 4,443 adolescents from ABCD Study Year 2 (2018–2020) and Year 4 (2020–2022)

FT_IC2.evidence: Exposure measured as 'recreational screen time' categorized as low (0–4), medium (>4–8), or high (>8) hours/day. This is generic total screen time composite with no smartphone-specific or social-media-specific breakdown. Criteria state: 'Generic total screen time or STS composite with no phone/SM breakdown' = NOT_MET (FT-IC2 exclusion). Per E17/E18 rules, unspecified screen-time variable → NOT_MET.

FT_IC3.evidence: FT-IC2 NOT_MET; subsequent criteria not assessed due to exposure failure

FT_IC4.evidence: FT-IC2 NOT_MET; subsequent criteria not assessed due to exposure failure

FT_IC5.evidence: FT-IC2 NOT_MET; subsequent criteria not assessed due to exposure failure

FT_IC6.evidence: FT-IC2 NOT_MET; subsequent criteria not assessed due to exposure failure

FT_EC7_reason: FT-IC2 NOT_MET; paper excluded on exposure criterion. FT-EC7 not evaluated.
notes: Paper examines associations between generic recreational screen time and cardiovascular risk outcomes. While original ABCD analysis with valid design and reported statistics (p<0.01, ARRs with 95% CIs), the exposure variable fails to meet FT-IC2 requirement for specific smartphone or social-media measurement. EXCLUDE.
